web.markdown:
# Text Corpus for NLP

![Datasets can help benchmark a model's performance. Source: Zhang and Wallace 2017, table 2.](https://devopedia.org/images/article/228/2676.1572257716.png)

Datasets can help benchmark a model's performance. Source: Zhang and Wallace 2017, table 2.[Zhang and Wallace 2017, table 2](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Zhang-and-Wallace-2017 "Zhang and Wallace 2017, table 2")

In the domain of natural language processing (NLP), statistical NLP in particular, there's a need to train the model or algorithm with lots of data. For this purpose, researchers have assembled many text corpora. A common corpus is also useful for benchmarking models.[Gurulingappa et al. 2012](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Gurulingappa-et-al.-2012 "Gurulingappa et al. 2012")

Typically, each text corpus is a collection of text sources. There are dozens of such corpora for a variety of NLP tasks. This article ignores speech corpora and considers only those in text form.

While English has many corpora, other natural languages too have their own corpora, though not as extensive as those for English. Using modern techniques, it's possible to apply NLP on low-resource languages, that is, languages with limited text corpora.[Ruder 2018a](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Ruder-2018a "Ruder 2018a")

## Discussion

- What are the traits of a good text corpus or wordlist?

It's said that a prototypical corpus must be machine-readable in Unicode. It must be a representative sample of the language in current use, balanced, and collected in natural settings.[Gries and Berez 2017, sec. 1.1](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Gries-and-Berez-2017 "Gries and Berez 2017, sec. 1.1")



A good corpus or wordlist must have the following traits:[Word Frequency 2019](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Word-Frequency-2019 "Word Frequency 2019")



  - **Depth**: A wordlist, for instance, should include the top 60K words and not just the top 3K words.
  - **Recent**: Corpus based on outdated texts is not going to suit today's tasks.
  - **Metadata**: Metadata should indicate the sources, assumptions, limitations and what's included in the corpus.
  - **Genre**: Unless corpus has been collected for specific tasks, it should include different genres such as newspapers, magazines, blogs, academic journals, etc.
  - **Size**: A corpus of half a million words or more ensures that low frequency words are also adequately represented.
  - **Clean**: A wordlist giving word forms of the same word can be messy to process. A better corpus would include only the lemma and part of speech.

- What are the different types of text corpora for NLP?

A plain text corpus is suitable for **unsupervised** training. Machine learning models learn from the data in an unsupervised manner. However, a corpus that has the raw text plus annotations can be used for **supervised** training.[Barba 2019](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Barba-2019 "Barba 2019") It takes considerable effort to create an annotated corpus but it may produce better results.




A corpus can be assembled from a variety of sources and genres. Such a corpus can be used for **general** NLP tasks. On the other hand, a corpus might be from a single source, domain or genre. Such a corpus can be used only for a **specific** purpose.[Evans 2019](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Evans-2019 "Evans 2019")

- What are the types of annotations that we can have on a text corpus?![American National Corpus Open annotated with POS, lemma and noun chunks, in XML and standalone form. Source: Gries and Berez 2017, fig. 6.](https://devopedia.org/images/article/228/6758.1572257741.png)

American National Corpus Open annotated with POS, lemma and noun chunks, in XML and standalone form. Source: Gries and Berez 2017, fig. 6.[Gries and Berez 2017, fig. 6](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Gries-and-Berez-2017 "Gries and Berez 2017, fig. 6")



Part-of-speech is one of the most common annotations because of its use in many downstream NLP tasks. Annotating with lemmas (base forms), syntactic parse trees (phrase-structure or dependency tree representations) and semantic information (word sense disambiguation) are also common.[Gries and Berez 2017, sec. 2.1](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Gries-and-Berez-2017 "Gries and Berez 2017, sec. 2.1") For discourse or text summarization tasks, annotations aid coreference resolutions.[Stanford NLP 2019a](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Stanford-NLP-2019a "Stanford NLP 2019a")[O'Donnell 2008, slide 6](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#O'Donnell-2008 "O'Donnell 2008, slide 6")



For instance, British Component of the International Corpus of English (ICE-GB) of 1 million words is POS tagged and syntactically parsed. Another parsed corpus in Penn Treebank. While WordNet and FrameNet are not corpora, they contain useful semantic information.[Gries and Berez 2017, sec. 2.1](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Gries-and-Berez-2017 "Gries and Berez 2017, sec. 2.1")



Audio/video recordings are transcribed and annotated as well. Annotations are phonetic (sounds), prosodic (variations), or interactional. Video transcripts may annotate for sign language and gesture.[Gries and Berez 2017, sec. 2.2](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Gries-and-Berez-2017 "Gries and Berez 2017, sec. 2.2")



Annotations could be **inline/embedded** with the text. When they appear on separate lines, it's called **multi-tiered** annotation. If they're in separate files, and linked to the text via hypertext, it's called **standalone** annotation.[Gries and Berez 2017, sec. 3](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Gries-and-Berez-2017 "Gries and Berez 2017, sec. 3")

- What are some NLP task-specific training corpora?![Example questions and answers from SQuAD. Source: SQuAD 2019b.](https://devopedia.org/images/article/228/2891.1572257763.png)

Example questions and answers from SQuAD. Source: SQuAD 2019b.[SQuAD 2019b](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#SQuAD-2019b "SQuAD 2019b")



Here are some task-specific corpora:




  - **POS Tagging**: Penn Treebank's WSJ section is tagged with a 45-tag tagset. Use Ritter dataset for social media content.[Ruder 2019c](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Ruder-2019c "Ruder 2019c")
  - **Named Entity Recognition**: CoNLL 2003 NER task is newswire content from Reuters RCV1 corpus. It considers four entity types. WNUT 2017 Emerging Entities task and OntoNotes 5.0 are other datasets.[Ruder 2019d](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Ruder-2019d "Ruder 2019d")
  - **Constituency Parsing**: Penn Treebank's WSJ section has dataset for this purpose.[Ruder 2019a](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Ruder-2019a "Ruder 2019a")
  - **Semantic role labelling**: OntoNotes v5.0 is useful due to syntactic and semantic annotations.[Ruder 2018b](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Ruder-2018b "Ruder 2018b")
  - **Sentiment Analysis**: IMDb has released 50K movie reviews.[Boukkouri 2018](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Boukkouri-2018 "Boukkouri 2018") Others are Amazon Customer Reviews of 130 million reviews,[Registry of Open Data 2019](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Registry-of-Open-Data-2019 "Registry of Open Data 2019") 6.7 million business reviews from Yelp,[Yelp 2019](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Yelp-2019 "Yelp 2019") and Sentiment140 of 160K tweets.[Cambridge Spark 2018](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Cambridge-Spark-2018 "Cambridge Spark 2018")
  - **Text Classification/Clustering**: Reuters-21578 is a collection of news documents from 1987 indexed by categories.[Brownlee 2017](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Brownlee-2017 "Brownlee 2017") 20 Newsgroups is another dataset of about 20K documents from 20 newsgroups.[Rennie 2008](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Rennie-2008 "Rennie 2008")
  - **Question Answering**: Stanford Question Answering Dataset (SQuAD) is a reading comprehension dataset with 100K questions plus 50K unanswerable questions.[SQuAD 2019](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#SQuAD-2019 "SQuAD 2019") Jeopardy dataset of about 200K Q&A is another example.[Ali 2019](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Ali-2019 "Ali 2019")

- Could you list some NLP text corpora by genre?

**Formal** genre is typically from books and academic journals. Examples are Project Gutenberg EBooks,[ODSC 2019](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#ODSC-2019 "ODSC 2019") Google Books Ngrams,[Ali 2019](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Ali-2019 "Ali 2019") and arXiv Bulk Data Access.[Iderhoff 2020](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Iderhoff-2020 "Iderhoff 2020") There are many text corpora from newswire. Examples are 20 Newsgroups and Reuters-21578.[Ali 2019](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Ali-2019 "Ali 2019")



For **informal** genre, we can include web data and emails. Corpora for these include Common Crawl,[Iderhoff 2020](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Iderhoff-2020 "Iderhoff 2020") Blogger Corpus, Wikipedia Links Data, Enron Emails, and UCI's Spambase. Corpora derived from reviews include Yelp Reviews, Amazon Customer Reviews, and IMDb Movie Reviews. Even more informal are SMS and tweets, for which we have Sentiment140, Twitter US Airline Sentiment, and SMS Spam Collection.[Ali 2019](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Ali-2019 "Ali 2019")



**Spoken language** is often different from written language. 2000 HUB5 English is a dataset that's a transcription of 40 telephone conversations.[Ali 2019](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Ali-2019 "Ali 2019") **Signed language** can also be annotated and transcribed to create a corpus.[Johnston 2008](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Johnston-2008 "Johnston 2008")



Since languages evolve, when analyzing **old text**, our models need to be trained likewise. Examples include DOE Corpus (600s-1150s),[Healey 2009](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Healey-2009 "Healey 2009") and COHA (1810s-2000s).[English Corpora 2019b](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#English-Corpora-2019b "English Corpora 2019b")



Another special case is of **learners** who are likely to express ideas differently. The Open Cambridge Learner Corpus contains 10K student responses of 2.9 million words.[Sketch Engine 2017](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Sketch-Engine-2017 "Sketch Engine 2017")



It's also common to have **domain-specific** corpora. For example, BioCreative and GENIA are for biology.[BioCreative 2019](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#BioCreative-2019 "BioCreative 2019")

- What are some generic training corpora for NLP?

Some of the well-known corpora are Brown Corpus, British National Corpus (BNC), Lancaster-Oslo/Beren Corpus (LOB), International Corpus of English (ICE),[Grigaliūnienė 2015, slide 5](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Grigali%C5%ABnien%C4%97-2015 "Grigaliūnienė 2015, slide 5") Corpus of Contemporary American English (COCA), Google Books Ngram Corpus,[Wikipedia 2019](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Wikipedia-2019 "Wikipedia 2019") Penn Treebank-3,[Marcus et al. 1999](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Marcus-et-al.-1999 "Marcus et al. 1999") English Gigaword Fifth Edition,[Parker et al. 2011](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Parker-et-al.-2011 "Parker et al. 2011") and OntoNotes Release 5.0.[Weischedel et al. 2013](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Weischedel-et-al.-2013 "Weischedel et al. 2013")



Wikipedia was not made for training NLP models but it can be used. We would need to strip markup. Gensim Python package has `gensim.corpora.wikicorpus.WikiCorpus` class to process Wikipedia data.[Mayo 2017](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Mayo-2017 "Mayo 2017")



Generic corpora are usually suited for **language modelling**, which is useful for other downstream tasks such as machine translation and speech recognition.[Brownlee 2017](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Brownlee-2017 "Brownlee 2017") Researchers have suggested using Project Gutenberg EBooks;[Brownlee 2017](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Brownlee-2017 "Brownlee 2017") Penn Treebank of about a million words pre-processed by Mikolov et al. in 2011; WikiText-2 of more than 2 million words; and WikiText-103. Google's one-billion word corpus provides a useful benchmark.[Ruder 2019e](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Ruder-2019e "Ruder 2019e")

- Derived from text corpus, which datasets are useful for NLP tasks?

**Wordlists** such as list of names or stopwords are useful for NLP work.[NLTK 2019](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#NLTK-2019 "NLTK 2019") Phrases in English (PIE) is another resource to explore distribution of words and phrases. It's based on the BNC corpus.[Fletcher 2011](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Fletcher-2011 "Fletcher 2011")



**Tagsets** are essential for POS tagging, chunking, dependency parsing or constituency parsing. [DKPro Core Tagset Reference](https://dkpro.github.io/dkpro-core/releases/1.9.0/docs/tagset-reference.html) is an excellent resource.[DKPro Core 2017](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#DKPro-Core-2017 "DKPro Core 2017") University of Lancaster maintains a multilingual semantic tagset.[UCREL 2019](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#UCREL-2019 "UCREL 2019")



**Treebanks** go beyond just POS-tagging a corpus. A treebank is an annotated corpus in which grammatical structure is typically represented as a tree structure. Examples are Penn Treebank and CHRISTINE Corpus.[Nivre 2007, sec. 1](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Nivre-2007 "Nivre 2007, sec. 1") Treebanks are useful for evaluating syntactic parsers or as resources for ML models to optimize linguistic analyzers.[Nivre 2007, sec. 4.2](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Nivre-2007 "Nivre 2007, sec. 4.2")



**Word embeddings** are real-valued vectors representations of words. These have improved many NLP task including language modelling and semantic analysis. While it's possible to learn embeddings from a large corpus, it's easier to start with downloadable embeddings.[Al-Rfou 2019](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Al-Rfou-2019 "Al-Rfou 2019") Two sources for downloads are [Polyglot](https://sites.google.com/site/rmyeid/projects/polyglot) and [Nordic Language Processing Laboratory (NLPL)](http://vectors.nlpl.eu/).




Perhaps by 2020, we'll be able to download pretrained **language models** and apply it to a variety of NLP tasks.[Ruder 2018a](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Ruder-2018a "Ruder 2018a")

- Which are some corpora for non-English languages?

For machine translation, it's common to have **parallel corpus**, that is, aligned text in multiple languages. We mention a few examples:[Ali 2019b](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Ali-2019b "Ali 2019b")



  - Aligned Hansards of the 36th Parliament of Canada containing 1.3 million pairs of aligned text segments in English and French
  - Europarl parallel corpus from 1996-2011 of 21 European languages from parliament proceedings
  - WMT 2014 EN-DE and WMT 2014 EN-FR[Ruder 2019b](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Ruder-2019b "Ruder 2019b")
  - A corpus using Wikipedia across 20 languages, 36 bitexts, about 610 million tokens and 26 million sentence fragments[OPUS 2019](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#OPUS-2019 "OPUS 2019")

An excellent source is [OPUS, the open parallel corpus](http://opus.nlpl.eu/). Lionbridge published a [list of parallel corpora](https://lionbridge.ai/datasets/25-best-parallel-text-datasets-for-machine-translation-training/) in 2019. Martin Weisser maintains a list that links to many [non-English corpora](http://martinweisser.org/corpora_site/corpora2.html).

## Milestones

1964

W. Nelson Francis and Henry Kučera at the Department of Linguistics, Brown University, publish a computer-readable general corpus to aid linguistic research on modern English. The corpus has 1 million words (500 samples of about 2000 words each). Revised editions appear later in 1971 and 1979. Called **Brown Corpus**, it inspires many other text corpora.[Kauhanen 2011](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Kauhanen-2011 "Kauhanen 2011") The corpus with annotations is included in Treebank-3 (1999).[Marcus et al. 1999](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Marcus-et-al.-1999 "Marcus et al. 1999")

1992

**Linguistic Data Consortium (LDC)** is formed to serve as a repository for NLP resources, including corpora. It's hosted at the University of Pennsylvania.[LDC 2019](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#LDC-2019 "LDC 2019")

1994

![BNC is a balanced corpus. Source: Wikipedia 2019b.](https://devopedia.org/images/article/228/9288.1572257789.png)

BNC is a balanced corpus. Source: Wikipedia 2019b.[Wikipedia 2019b](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Wikipedia-2019b "Wikipedia 2019b")

A 100-million corpus of British English called **BNC (British National Corpus)** is assembled between 1991 and 1994. It's balanced across genres. A follow-up task called **BNC2014** is started in 2014, which can help in understanding how language evolves. Spoken BNC2014 is released in September 2017.[Wikipedia 2019b](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Wikipedia-2019b "Wikipedia 2019b") Written BNC2014 is expected to come out in 2019.[CASS 2019](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#CASS-2019 "CASS 2019")

1999

**Penn Treebank-3** is released. It's based upon the original Treebank (1992) and its revised Treebank II (1995). This work started in 1989 at the University of Pennsylvania.[Marcus 2011](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Marcus-2011 "Marcus 2011") Treebank-3 includes tagged/parsed Brown Corpus, 1 million words of 1989 WSJ material annotated in Treebank II style, tagged sample of ATIS-3, and tagged/parsed Switchboard Corpus.[Marcus et al. 1999](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Marcus-et-al.-1999 "Marcus et al. 1999") Apart from POS tags, the corpus includes chunk tags, relation tags and anchor tags.[CLiPS 2018](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#CLiPS-2018 "CLiPS 2018") The **BLLIP 1987-89 WSJ Corpus Release 1** has 30 million words and supplements the WSJ section of Treebank-3.[Charniak et al. 2000](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Charniak-et-al.-2000 "Charniak et al. 2000")

2008

Collected for the years 1990-2007, the **Corpus of Contemporary American English (COCA)** is released with 365 million words. By December 2017, it has 560 million words, adding 20 million each year. There's good balance of spoken, fiction, popular magazines, newspapers, and academic texts. It's been noted that COCA contains many common words that are missing in the American National Corpus (ANC), a corpus of 22 million words.[BYU 2019](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#BYU-2019 "BYU 2019")[English Corpora 2019](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#English-Corpora-2019 "English Corpora 2019")

Jun

2011

**English Gigaword Fifth Edition** is released by LDC. It comes from seven English newswire services. It has 4 billion words and takes up 26 gigabytes uncompressed.[Parker et al. 2011](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Parker-et-al.-2011 "Parker et al. 2011") The first edition appeared in 2003.[Graff and Cieri 2003](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Graff-and-Cieri-2003 "Graff and Cieri 2003") In November 2012, researchers at the John Hopkins University add syntactic and discourse structure annotations to this corpus after parsing more than 183 million sentences.[Napoles et al. 2012](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Napoles-et-al.-2012 "Napoles et al. 2012")

Jul

2012

From digitized books, Google releases version 2 of **Google Books Ngrams**. Version 1 came out in July 2009. Only n-grams that appear more than 40 times are included. The corpus includes 1-gram to 5-grams. It includes many non-English languages as well. To experiment on small sets of phrases, researchers can try out the online [Google Books Ngram Viewer](https://books.google.com/ngrams).[Google Books 2012](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Google-Books-2012 "Google Books 2012")

Aug

2012

As a corpus for informal genre, **English Web Treebank (EWT)** is released by LDC. This includes content from weblogs, reviews, question-answers, newsgroups, and email. It has about 250K word-level tokens and 16K sentence-level tokens. It's annotated for POS and syntactic structure. This includes Enron Corporation emails from 1999-2002.[Bies et al. 2012](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Bies-et-al.-2012 "Bies et al. 2012") In 2014, Silveira et al. provide annotation of syntactic dependencies for this corpus that can be used to train dependency parsers.[Silveira et al. 2014](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Silveira-et-al.-2014 "Silveira et al. 2014")

Sep

2019

**Common Crawl** publishes 240 TiB of uncompressed data from 2.55 billion web pages. Of these, 1 billion URLs were not present in previous crawls.[Nagel 2019](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Nagel-2019 "Nagel 2019") Common Crawl started in 2008. In 2013, they moved from ARC to Web ARChive (WARC) file format. WAT files contain the metadata. WET file contain plaintext of the WARC files.[Common Crawl 2019](https://devopedia.org/text-corpus-for-nlp#Common-Crawl-2019 "Common Crawl 2019")

# CORAA Versions

### [Copy heading link](https://sites.google.com/view/tarsila-c4ai/coraa-versions\#h.vfilh410uo9e)    CORAA  (CORpus de Áudios Anotados)

A large multi-purpose corpus of Brazilian Portuguese audio files aligned with transcriptions and manually validated for the purpose of training ASR and TTS models and also Sentiment Analysisusing acoustic audio features.

Version 1:  CORAA ASR - Academic Corpora Projects (finished work in September 15 2021)

[CORAA ASR version 1.1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10579-022-09621-4) is composed of academic corpora projects and a collection of TeD talks; all of them with academic license (CC BY NC ND 4.0 International).

- [Nurc-Recife](https://fale.ufal.br/projeto/nurcdigital/) (OLIVEIRA JR, 2017) contains spontaneous and prepared speech from Recife - PE;

- [SP2010](https://projetosp2010.fflch.usp.br/)(MENDES, 2011) contains  spontaneous and read speech from the interior of São Paulo;

- [ALIP](https://www.alip.ibilce.unesp.br/projeto) (GONÇALVES, 2019) contains  spontaneous speech from the interior of São Paulo; and

- [C-ORAL Brasil I](https://www.c-oral-brasil.org/) (RASO & MELLO, 2012) contains spontaneous speech from Minas Gerais;

- [TeD Talks](https://www.ted.com/about/programs-initiatives/tedx-program) collection contains 72.74 hours of prepared speech taken from TeDx Talks in Portuguese.


ALIP,  SP2010, C-ORAL Brasil, and NURC-RECIFE currently total 228.05 hours; all the datasets together total290.79hours. Theywere manually validated for training ASR models for Portuguese, using the [BrazSpeechData Platform](http://143.107.183.175:14888/login).

This dataset is available at [https://github.com/nilc-nlp/CORAA/](https://github.com/nilc-nlp/CORAA/) to be part of the [Shared Task on on ASR for spontaneous and prepared speech](https://sites.google.com/view/tarsila-c4ai/shared-tasks).

Version 2: CORAA NURC-SP (finished work in December 15 2023)

NURC-SP contains spontaneous and prepared speech from São Paulo, capital, divided in a part with audios and manual transcriptions (47 inquiries) and audios only (328 inquiries). NURC-SP was divided in three subcorpora in the Tarsila project:  Córpus Mínimo, CATNA (Córpus de Áudios Transcritos não Anotados) and Córpus de Áudios:

- Córpus Mínimo. The dataset of 21 files (~ 19 hours) of the part of NURC-SP corpus composed of audios and manual transcriptions was automatically aligned using the set of tools for forced alignment [aeneas](https://pypi.org/project/aeneas/) (https://www.readbeyond.it/aeneas/). This dataset, called Córpus Mínimo, wasmanually  segmented according to prosodic criteria based on the work by  Raso and Mello (2012). It is available at Portulan Clarin repository under CC BY-NC-ND 4.0 license - https://hdl.handle.net/21.11129/0000-000F-73CA-C

- CATNA (Córpus de Áudios Transcritos não Anotados). The dataset is composed of 26 files (~ 24 hours) (audios and transcriptions non-aligned), in which 21 of them were automatically segmented by an automatic prosodic segmentation method based on Biron et al. (2021), customized for Brazilian Portuguese (https://github.com/nilc-nlp/ProsSegue); 5 of them were automatically aligned using the forced alignment tool [aeneas](https://pypi.org/project/aeneas/) (https://www.readbeyond.it/aeneas/).The automatic segmentations were revised by students who finished the revision on May 31 2024.

- Córpus de Áudios. The dataset of 328 audios (~ 239.30 valid hours) were automatically transcribed by [WhisperX](https://github.com/m-bain/whisperX) which provides fast transcription using the large-v2 model of [Whisper](https://openai.com/research/whisper)  and diarization via [pyannote-audio](https://github.com/pyannote/pyannote-audio).  Automatic transcripts were reviewed by a number of students varying from 6 to 18  from June to December 2023.


This dataset is available at the NURC-SP DIGITAL Portal for linguistic analysis: [http://tarsila.icmc.usp.br:8080/nurc/home](http://tarsila.icmc.usp.br:8080/nurc/home).

The NURC-SP Audio Corpus was also prepared for evaluating the ASR task: [https://github.com/nilc-nlp/nurc-sp-audio-corpus](https://github.com/nilc-nlp/nurc-sp-audio-corpus)

Version 3: CORAAPrograma Certas Palavras (digitalization completed, finished  work in May 31 2024)

- [Programa Certas Palavras](https://cedae.iel.unicamp.br/guia.php?view=details&id=6512bd43d9caa6e02c990b0a82652dca) Série 4 (from CEDAE, UNICAMP), containing  133 K7 tapes and 6 roll tapes;  and Série 1 (from CEDAE, UNICAMP) containing 10 roll tapes.


The “Certas Palavras” program was conceived in 1980, on the initiative of journalists Claudiney José Ferreira and Jorge Marques de Vasconcellos, and was implemented in the form of a program broadcast on radio, dealing with books and ideas. CORAACertas Palavras dataset contains ~ 63 hours of valid spontaneous speech divided in 163episodes. The episodes were automatically transcribed by [WhisperX](https://github.com/m-bain/whisperX%29) which provides fast transcription using the large-v2 model of [Whisper](https://openai.com/research/whisper)  and diarization via [pyannote-audio](https://github.com/pyannote/pyannote-audio). Two students revised the automatic transcriptions from November 2023 to March 2024 and 12 students revised the diarization labels of pyannote-audio for the real speaker names (from April to May 2024), resulting a resource for evaluating ASR, TTS and diarization methods.

License: CC BY-NC 4.0

[https://github.com/GustavoEvangelistaAraujo/CertasPalavras/](https://github.com/GustavoEvangelistaAraujo/CertasPalavras/)

Dataset: [https://huggingface.co/datasets/nilc-nlp/certas\_palavras/](https://huggingface.co/datasets/nilc-nlp/certas_palavras/)

Version 4: CORAA MuPe life-stories (automatic transcription revision work completed on April 2024)

- 289life-stories of MuPe collection (365valid hours) were  processed and anonymized to be part of our corpus.

- The life-storieswere automatically transcribed by [WhisperX](https://github.com/m-bain/whisperX) which provides fast transcription using the large-v2 model of [Whisper](https://openai.com/research/whisper) and diarization via [pyannote-audio](https://github.com/pyannote/pyannote-audio). Ten students revised the automatic transcriptions from June 2023 to April  2024. The evaluation of pyannote-audio diarization labels was carried out in May 2024 with two trained students, throughout the life history available at https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ctNiVFxcep0#. This life story was divided into 1,146 segments by WhisperX and Cohen's Kappa for 2 raters was 0.947, considered almost perfect (Landis & Koch, 1977). Therefore, we consider automatic diarization labels suitable for use in training ASRs and TTS models.


Version 5: CORAA SOFIA-FALA (ongoing work)

Corpus based on the [Sofia Fala project](https://dcm.ffclrp.usp.br/sofiafala/)  (MELONI et al., 2021, Souza et al., 2019, Stella et al., 2019, Souza et al., 2018, Andrade et al., 2000, WERTZNER 2000):

-  Impaired speech corpus in the capture phase

- Website for the donation of 10 audios captured by the reading of 10 sentences per person with speech disorder:  [https://dcm.ffclrp.usp.br/coleta/html/inicio.php](https://dcm.ffclrp.usp.br/coleta/html/inicio.php)

- Rules and ethical aspects for donation following LGPD: TCLE and TCUISV specific for donation

- To be used in an ASR for impaired speech Shared-Task toadvance methods and applications to help on speech delay, phonological disorder, phonetic disorder, childhood speech apraxia, dysarthria, associated or not with a diagnosis of Down syndrome, autism spectrum disorder and intellectual deficit.


Version 6: CORAA SER  Sentiment Analysis Dataset  (finished work in September 15 2021)

[CORAA SER version 1.0](https://github.com/rmarcacini/ser-coraa-pt-br/) is composed of approximately 50 minutes of audio segments labeled in three classes: [neutral, non-neutral female, and non-neutral male](https://colab.research.google.com/drive/1Q89U0d2-i3ILy7-r6BMvbfVI3gPkvZZz?usp=sharing). While the neutral class represents audio segments with no well-defined emotional state, the non-neutral classes represent segments associated with one of the primary emotional states in the speaker's speech.

This dataset was built from the C-ORAL-BRASIL I corpus (Raso and Mello, 2012). The available corpus consists of audio segments representing Brazilian Portuguese informal spontaneous speech. The non-neutral emotion class was labeled considering paralinguistic elements (laughing, crying, etc).

This dataset is available at [https://github.com/rmarcacini/ser-coraa-pt-br/](https://github.com/rmarcacini/ser-coraa-pt-br/) to be part of the [Shared-Task on Speech Emotion Recognition with Acoustic Aspects](https://sites.google.com/view/tarsila-c4ai/shared-tasks)

web.markdown:
# Datasets

Here is a list of some publicly available speech/text datasets that I gathered in the last 4 years, mainly in PT-BR.

I hope this helps you :)

## Speech

- CETUC - [Download](http://www02.smt.ufrj.br/~igor.quintanilha/alcaim.tar.gz)
  - CETUC dataset \[1\] contains almost 145 hours of speech signals performed by 50 male and 50 female speakers, each one pronouncing 1,000 phonetically balanced sentences selected from the CETEN-Folha corpus \[38\]. The CETUC dataset was recorded in acontrolled environment at a sampling rate of 16kHz.
- LapsBM - Download [part 1](http://www02.smt.ufrj.br/~igor.quintanilha/lapsbm-val.tar.gz) \| [part 2](http://www02.smt.ufrj.br/~igor.quintanilha/lapsbm-test.tar.gz)
  - LapsBM1.4 \[2\] is a dataset used by the Fala Brasil group of Fed- eral University of Pará to evaluate large vocabulary continuous speech recognition (LVCSR) system in Brazilian Portuguese. It contains 35 speakers (10 women), each one with 20 unique utterances, totaling 700 utterances. Audio has been recorded at 22.05 kHz without environment control.
- Sidney - [Download](http://www02.smt.ufrj.br/~igor.quintanilha/sid.tar.gz)
  - Kindly provided by Dr. Sidney dos Santos for research purposes, this dataset contains recordings by 72 speakers (20 women) from 17 to 59 years old with fields such as place of birth, age, gender, education, and occupation. Recorded at 22.05kHz in a non-controlled environment, its 5,777 utterances were transcribed at word level without time alignment. Contents span from spoken digits, single words, complex sequences, spelling of name, and local of birth to phonetic covering, and semantically unpredictable sentences. Some excerpts were discarded due to a systematic transcription error found.
- VoxForge - [Download](http://www02.smt.ufrj.br/~igor.quintanilha/voxforge-ptbr.tar.gz)
  - VoxForge \[3\] is the most heterogeneous corpus. The idea of VoxForge is to distribute transcribed speech audio under GPL license, facilitating the development of acoustic models. Everyone can record specific utterances and send to them. Their Portuguese Brazilian language section contains at least 111 speakers not all having information about genre or age. The audio files have been recorded at different sample rates ranging from 16 kHz to 44.1 kHz, and many records are in low-quality, presenting low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR). A total of 4,130 utterances were transcribed at word level for Brazilian Portuguese.

|  |  | Words | Words |  | Speakers |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Dataset | Subset | Hours | Total | Unique | M | F |
| BRSD v1 | Sid | 7:23 | 33,189 | 5,676 | 52 | $20$ |
|  | VoxForge | 4:14 | 20,879 | 729 | 111 |  |
|  | Spoltech | 1:35 | 16,776 | 558 | 477 |  |
|  | LapsBM | 0:54 | 7,228 | 2,731 | 25 | 10 |
|  | Total | 14:07 | 78,072 | 7,772 | 595 |  |
| BRSD v2 | CETUC | 144:39 | 1,040,278 | 3,528 | 50 | $50$ |
|  | Total | 158:47 | 1,118,350 | 8,328 | 695 |  |

## Text

- LapsNews - [Download](http://www02.smt.ufrj.br/~igor.quintanilha/laps-folha-1.0.txt)
  - LapsNews \[4\] is a text corpus dataset consisting of automatic crawling of the top ten daily Brazilian newspapers available on the Internet in 2010. It was post-processed to convert to lowercase letters, to remove tags and punctuation marks, and to expand numbers and well-know acronyms to the written form, resulting in a corpus with approximately 120k sentences.
- CETENFolha - [Download](http://www02.smt.ufrj.br/~igor.quintanilha/ceten.xml)
  - CETENFolha \[5\] dataset is a corpus containing over 24 million Brazilian words crawled from the 1994 editions of Folha de São Paulo newspaper, resulting in approximately 1.6M sentences.
- WikiText PT-BR - [Download](http://www02.smt.ufrj.br/~igor.quintanilha/ptwiki-20181125.txt)
  - WikiText PT-BR dataset is a collection of over 8 million sentences extracted from the Wikipedia articles and was built for this work. The dataset is available under Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike license. The dataset was pre-processed to remove all punctuation and tags and convert numbers into their written form, to be well suited for ASR models.

|  |  | Words |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Datasets | #sentences | Total | Unique |
| LapsNews | 119k | 2.7M | 66k |
| CETENFolha | 1.5M | 26M | 214k |
| WikiText PT-BR | 8.5M | 194M | 1.1M |
| **Total** | 10.2M | 223M | 1.2M |

## References

1. V. F. S. Alencar and A. Alcaim, “LSF and LPC - derived features forlarge vocabulary distributed continuous speech recognition in Brazilian Portuguese,” in Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems and Computers, October 2008, pp. 1237–124;
2. “Falabrasil - UFPA”, [https://github.com/falabrasil/gitlab-resources](https://github.com/falabrasil/gitlab-resources), accessed: 2020-03-19;
3. “Voxforge”, [https://http://www.voxforge.org](https://http//www.voxforge.org), accessed: 2020-03-19;
4. N. Neto, C. Patrick, A. Klautau, and I. Trancoso, “Free tools andresources for brazilian portuguese speech recognition, ”Journal of theBrazilian Computer Society, vol. 17, no. 1, pp. 53–68, November 2011;
5. Linguateca, “CETENFolha”, [https://www.linguateca.pt/cetenfolha/index\_info.html](https://www.linguateca.pt/cetenfolha/index_info.html), accessed: 2020-03-19.

# NLP — Datasets em português

[![Pierre Guillou](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:32:32/1*kXdLq_2eKCVQx04JB4Dh-w.jpeg)](https://medium.com/@pierre_guillou?source=post_page---byline--7e1790a44d42---------------------------------------)

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:1000/1*Q1MsLAg1xw1INRNRHGtsXA.png)

## Sobre a necessidade de modelos de linguagem não apenas em inglês

Mesmo que o inglês seja hoje a língua mais falada no mundo ( [cerca de 1,2 bilhões de pessoas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_countries_by_English-speaking_population)), **o mundo é multilíngue**(por exemplo, existem [34 línguas com 45 milhões ou mais de falantes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_languages_by_total_number_of_speakers) na edição de 2019 do [Ethnologue](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ethnologue), uma referência linguística publicada por [SIL International).](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SIL_International)

É necessário, portanto, **ter modelos de linguagem natural treinados em todas as línguas existentes**, e não apenas em inglês, uma vez que esses modelos constituem a base essencial para o treinamento de modelos capazes de realizar uma determinada tarefa em linguística (classificação, Q&A, síntese, pesquisa de entidades , etc.).

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![Este é um diagrama codificado por cores para indicar a porcentagem de falantes de inglês em quase todos os países do mundo.](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:1000/0*8VJ94nQKA89JMIK9.png)

Este é um diagrama codificado por cores para indicar a porcentagem de falantes de inglês em quase todos os países do mundo. Algumas pequenas ilhas não foram contabilizadas. (fonte da imagem: [lista de países por população de língua inglesa na Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_countries_by_English-speaking_population))

No entanto, se for extremamente simples e gratuito baixar um modelo de idioma treinado em inglês por meio da [biblioteca Transformers do Hugging Face](https://huggingface.co/transformers/), por exemplo, geralmente é muito mais difícil encontrar online um modelo treinado em outro idioma.

## Get Pierre Guillou’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

Subscribe

Subscribe

Remember me for faster sign in

**Uma solução possível?** Para cada idioma falado no mundo, **publicar conjuntos de dados (datasets) de corpus linguísticos em código aberto (Open Source)**, permitindo **treinar desde o início** modelos de linguagem natural ou **ajustar (fine-tune) esses modelos pré-treinados para realizar tarefas de PLN** (Processamento de Linguagem Natural ou NLP em inglês) específicas como a classificação, perguntas e respostas, NER...

## NLP em português

No caso do português, essa lista de conjuntos de dados de NLP (PNL) existe online no [fórum do AI Lab](https://forum.ailab.unb.br/) e é regularmente enriquecida :-)

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![fórum do AI Lab](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*2_ct1m9XcSbKztugMF6Q9g.png)

[fórum do AI Lab](https://forum.ailab.unb.br/)

De fato, **uma categoria foi aberta****(**[**Datasets em português**](https://forum.ailab.unb.br/t/datasets-em-portugues/251)**)****e permite que todos publiquem um post com a descrição e o link para seu conjunto de dados em português**.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![Categoria Datasets em português no fórum do AI Lab](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*Tpi_yNeTJXTBtxqDYj_ZZw.png)

Categoria [Datasets em português](https://forum.ailab.unb.br/t/datasets-em-portugues/251) no fórum do AI Lab

Nessa lista, vocês já podem encontrar os links para baixar corpus linguísticos em português de vários GB como **Wikipedia**, **OSCAR**, **BrWaC**, bem como os famosos conjuntos de dados **SQuAD** e **GLUE**… em português também!

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![Alguns datasets em português já presentes no fórum do AI Lab](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:1000/1*B-vSxiHEl9OuJG5LmhEWyA.png)

[Alguns datasets em português](https://forum.ailab.unb.br/t/datasets-em-portugues/251) já presentes no fórum do AI Lab

## Agradecimentos

Gostaria de agradecer particularmente aos primeiros doadores de links para conjuntos de dados em português e de código aberto : Monique Monteiro, e Juliana Resplande, Pedro Vitor Quinta de Castro e Ruan Chaves Rodrigues do grupo [Deep Learning Brasil](http://www.deeplearningbrasil.com.br/). Também tive grande prazer em participar da elaboração dessa lista.

Por fim, gostaria de agradecer ao [AI Lab](https://ailab.unb.br/) (UnB) que, ao disponibilizar esse fórum de Deep Learning em português para todos, possibilitou a publicação dessa lista de datasets de NLP em português.

![AI Lab (Universidade de Brasília — UnB, Brasil)](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:200/0*uBM2iKAxgI6X_kPg.jpeg)

[AI Lab](https://ailab.unb.br/) (Universidade de Brasília — UnB, Brasil)

**Sobre o autor**: Pierre Guillou é consultor de IA no Brasil e na França, pesquisador em Deep Learning e NLP do [AI Lab](https://ailab.unb.br/) (Unb) e professor de Inteligência Artificial (UnB). Entre em contato com ele por meio de seu [perfil no LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/in/pierreguillou/).
